title: ResearchSlop
author: Judd Antin
text:
ResearchSlop
If you’re paying attention to the research community POV on AI, you might think everyone’s on board. But every time I see another breathless panel about AI-powered research tools, I see researchers smiling with their mouths and not with their eyes.
Recently Anil Dash articulated this general AI unreality better than I can:
Technologies like LLMs have utility, but the absurd way they’ve been over-hyped, the fact they’re being forced on everyone, and the insistence on ignoring the many valid critiques about them make it very difficult to focus on legitimate uses where they might add value.
What’s amazing is the reality that virtually 100% of tech experts I talk to in the industry feel this way, yet nobody outside of that cohort will mention this reality.
(Source — The Majority AI View by Anil Dash)
Dash is spot on. And at the risk of being the metaphorical old man publicly shaking his fist, I find this strong parallel:
AI powered research tools are useful. I’m glad we have them. In the right hands, they’re powerful assistants for specific research tasks.
But they regularly produce bad outputs along with the good ones. They’re over-hyped and deeply flawed. AI tools cannot replace researchers, and the strong mandates to try are misguided. In the hands of non-researchers, AI research tools often do more harm than good.
Everyone I trust in research thinks some version of this. All the most expert and senior people. But somehow our public conversations still lean towards eager cheerleading. What is happening?
It’s past time for the research community to say the quiet part loud. Openly talking about the fact that AI tools just aren’t that good isn’t clutching our pearls, its owning our fate.
We need to proactively inject more reality into the ongoing conversation about the role of AI tools in research for research to thrive as a discipline. We need a pointed view of the pros and cons. And we need to export a stronger POV to non-research decision makers in search of efficiencies. That conversation should start with ResearchSlop.
Bad Research
ResearchSlop is research that’s skin deep. Don’t look too close, and it appears insightful and rigorous. But it’s not. ResearchSlop is a set of superficial, poorly justified outputs with a sexy but thin veneer of credibility pasted on top.
It’s a beautiful idiot. It’s a shiny turd.
Here are a few examples of ResearchSlop in practice:
- Designer does a usability test with their colleague, proclaims the UX excellent.
- PM calls a few key clients, revises the strategy to reflect “the voice of the customer.”
- Marketing fields a large survey that’s leading and biased, confirms executives’ belief that customers will buy the new product.
- CEO asks his family and friends what they think of his great idea, concludes it is in fact great.
We used to just call this bad research, but ResearchSlop, especially in 2025, has evolved into something more insidious.
Slop, WorkSlop, ResearchSlop
If you’ve done much late night doomscrolling, you’ve probably encountered slop. Slop is “digital content made with generative AI, specifically when perceived to show a lack of effort, quality or deeper meaning. (source)” It’s glossy and compelling, but it barely hangs together.
And from slop we get WorkSlop, a term introduced recently by Kate Niederhoffer and her colleagues in an excellent HBR article. WorkSlop is “AI generated work content that masquerades as good work, but lacks the substance to meaningfully advance a given task.”
WorkSlop helps explain a powerful contradiction — if AI tools are supposedly enabling incredible speed and efficiency, why is there so little evidence of it?
The researchers’ answer:
“The insidious effect of WorkSlop is that it shifts the burden of the work downstream, requiring the receiver to interpret, correct, or redo the work.”
In the end it’s more efficient for the producer, but much less efficient overall. It takes more time and effort to clean up the WorkSlop than to do it right from the start.
This.
Bad research has always been a thing, but now we have tools that allow anyone to produce sexy, terrible research at lightning speed. They never need to encounter an expert in the process. Their results benefit from the perceived credibility of AI and a large sample. But in the end, the insights are misleading and wrong. The work, the product, the business suffers.
In the end, a turd is still a turd.
A Different Conversation
It’s past time to balance AI optimism with a realistic take on where research actually goes from here. AI research tools are a part of our future, and moving past cheerleading to reality will get us there faster.
So many research teams are being dictated to by their leadership. Cut the people, scale with AI, go faster, democratize all the tools. Going all-in on AI feels like good stakeholder management and saving our jobs. I understand why that makes it tough for many people to speak up. But that is exactly why public conversations are so important.
And let’s not give executives too much credit. AI mandates are hitting every function, not because they necessarily make sense, but because they’re the only thing that makes the balance sheet make sense. How else can we justify blowing nine figures on data centers and AI tools?
Researchers arefacing the same WorkSlop-powered reality that engineers are. Sentiment is turning against vibe coding because it’s not very good. Engineers are worried about AI tools that hallucinate, introduce security vulnerabilities, and produce flawed code that’s time consuming to fix.
Just to put a fine point on it, here’s a story a senior research colleague of mine shared:
A trusted PM used ran dozens of AI-moderated interviews and synthesized the results. The insights looked great, and the PM presented them confidently. My colleague, unaware, looked into the study and found a wide variety of flaws, mistakes, and hallucinations which meant the insights were useless. They pointed them out to the PM, who had no idea. Then the research team re-ran the study, and produced very different outputs which actually drove the needed product decisions.
I’ve had many private conversations with researchers that contain stories like this. Dealing with hallucinations, inappropriate or unjustified themes, missed insights, confused business or product context, or biased methodology is constant. The result is insights and recommendations that are useless at best, actively misleading at worst. Walking back from ResearchSlop is taking a lot of time.
These conversations are also full of useful wisdom about how to properly position AI tools — when they work, when they don’t, and what guardrails are needed. But those conversations are too often happening in private.
I get why. At this point in the hype-cycle, it can be scary to contradict the rosy AI narrative. But it’s time to get out in front and remind everyone of two big points. First, ResearchSlop is insidious and terrible if we care about product and business outcomes. Second, it doesn’t have to be this way.
We need researchers publicly sharing not just which AI tools they’re using, but where those tools fell short. We need transparent discussions about combatting the pressure to scale with AI rather than headcount. We need more specific sharing of case studies and cautionary tales of when AI tools produced ResearchSlop, and what researchers did about it. These things are happening, but not nearly enough.
The goal isn’t to be anti-AI or to gatekeep research. It’s to create space for the reasonable majority — the expert practitioners who see both the value and the limitations — to shape how these tools get integrated into practice.
Moving Upstream
The public conversation can help position researchers inside their companies, but we should also be working with AI tool producers to evolve the tools we need. The faster these tools improve and adapt to what we need, the better off we’ll all be. Many companies are already eager to engage the research community as a path to signed contracts, but fewer are genuinely partnering with us on the path forward.
Some of these founders are also outright delusional, convinced that researchers will soon be obsolete because the tools will do all the work. After all, there’s a truckload of money to be made by selling research tools directly to non-researchers — PMs, designers, marketers, anyone with a budget and some confirmation bias to fulfill.
But this isn’t just wrong, it’s short-sighted. In reality, by enabling ResearchSlop, AI research tools only hasten the near-term outcome that vibe coding tools are currently suffering through — over-promising and under-delivering breeds skepticism, mistrust, and rejection.
Sadly, even more modest nods to reality can be a hard sell. In conversations with several AI research tool companies, I’ve pushed on the need for thoughtful and robust constraints built into the products themselves. Right now, most AI tool producers are reluctant to tell paying customers what they can’t do. As a result, these tools will happily let users ship biased surveys, skip critical research steps, or overly-rely on AI synthesis without much more than a mild warning.
But they’re making the classic mistake of focusing on the output (glossy research materials) rather than the outcome (insights that improve products). Explicitly working with the community to develop outcome-focused, ResearchSlop-free tools is a path to market share and customer retention.
Figma is a great example of doing this well. They’ve explicitly positioned their tools as augmenting rather than replacing designers. Of course, people still use Figma to produce slop, but they’ve designed the tools with something else in mind.
I am decidedly not suggesting we clutch our pearls, and insist that there can be no useful research without researchers. But context matters, and I would argue that AI tools that are smartly expert-in-the-loop will be the ones that win in an increasingly crowded marketplace.
The Path Forward
The research community is at yet another inflection point. AI tools aren’t going away, and neither is the pressure for speed, scale, and efficiency. But we get to decide whether the next chapter is defined by ResearchSlop — fast, shiny, and fundamentally wasteful — or by something better.
None of this is about rejecting AI or clinging to the old ways, despite the enduring image of the old man shaking his fist. It’s about ensuring that when organizations adopt AI tools, they actually get what they’re paying for: research that drives good decisions rather than research that creates more work downstream.
The private conversations show our community already has the realistic knowledge about how to move forward. The reasonable majority of researchers already understand this. Now we need to make sure everyone else does too.
Special thanks to my amazing colleagues who contributed incredible feedback on this article. Who knows if they’d like to be associated with this? 😜 So I won’t name them, but they know, and they are great.
{EDIT — The amazing Jess Holbrook wrote an article about ResearchSlop at almost exactly the same time. Uncanny! It’s full of wonderful, complementary ideas. Take a look!}
I’m an executive coach — feel free to reach out. Send me an email at judd@juddantin.com. I write about leadership, product, design, and management. Check out my newsletter One Big Thought. Sign up to get email updates here. And Remember that No One Has Any Idea What They’re Doing.
